feedback.text: An act like this, regulating the conduct of businesses on the internet while respecting the freedom of private users, is in dire need. As a further consideration, I would suggest expanding this act to also encompass the status of digital goods. Companies often change the supposed state of their sold products between service and good, depending on whichever benefits them the most at the time, while never being clear to the consumer about what they truly purchase. Other products are a confusing mix of both service and good, such as Square Enix' "Final Fantasy 14: A Realm Reborn", a always online multiplayer game. To play the game, you must first purchase a "base game" for a lump sum, and then purchase "play time" (basically, a subscription) to access the content of the previously purchased game. On top of this, various microtransactions are offered as well (cash shop), mostly cosmetics, but also things like "Eternal Bonding" ceremonies, a type of in-game marriage between two player characters. Additionally, Square Enix offers a free trial of the game, including the base game and two expansions, which can be played without a time limit, albeit in a limited state. Once a consumer purchases the base game, which is required to buy and access the newest expansions not included in the free trial, these previously free portions of the game cannot be accessed at all anymore without an active subscription. Which directives and laws concerning goods and service, and their distinction, apply to a game like this? If a consumer pays a lump sum for a game, wouldn't they be entitled to some form of the game? If the game is treated wholly as a service, what does the consumer even purchase by "buying" the base game? What about items purchased from the cash shop? What happens to someone's purchases when the servers shut down? (Please note that this is merely an example, and I do not question the legality of Square Enix' business.) Questions like this, to me, would be important to answer, and the topic of digital goods is definitely one related to Digital Fairness.
